metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `refused` with the actual API key in the blast-radius signal definition: assert `verdict.allowed is False` (BreakerVerdict) or `census_record['breaker']['allowed'] is False` (census dict) — confirm against classify.py:294-375.
findings.finding: The plan's blast-radius signal asserts 'the `refused` census/verdict the breaker emits', but the actual codebase API uses `BreakerVerdict.allowed` (bool) and `census()['breaker']['allowed']` (dict key) — the term `refused` does not appear anywhere in classify.py, and no `refused` field exists in the emitted census record. A harness asserting a non-existent `refused` key will silently pass or fail for the wrong reason.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, 'Blast-radius breaker' bullet; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/classify.py:294-375
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Blast-radius breaker' signal bullet, change '…asserted via the `refused` census/verdict the breaker emits…' to '…asserted via `BreakerVerdict.allowed is False` (or `census(...)['breaker']['allowed'] is False`) from `classify.check_blast_radius` / `classify.census`…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the C4 monkey-patch target against lambswool-shaded-eider's actual implementation site: per the ticket, C4 modifies the _run_acli subprocess path (not _call_with_backoff which already handles Retry-After); update the signal definition to name the subprocess retry path as the primary target.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition asserts the default monkey-patch target is 'the REST-style acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff (reads headers ~line 267)', framing this as what C4 implements. But _call_with_backoff uses urllib.error.HTTPError to catch HTTP responses — it is the urllib/REST path, not the ACLI subprocess path. The C4 ticket (lambswool-shaded-eider) is explicitly about the _run_acli SUBPROCESS path and states 'All ACLI calls go through the _run_acli subprocess ... which sees only returncode + text stdout/stderr.' The plan then hedges 'If C4 lands only on the plain subprocess path, patch that' — but given the ticket, C4 almost certainly lands on the subprocess path, making the 'default target' assertion misleading. The harness may patch the wrong call site.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4: 'Default target: the REST-style acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff (reads headers ~line 267)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the C4 patch target: `_call_with_backoff` already handles 429/Retry-After (it is the REST/urllib path); C4 adds handling to the **plain subprocess `_run_acli` path** — make `_run_acli` (or the retry wrapper C4 installs around it) the primary monkey-patch target, and drop the 'Default target' framing that names the already-implemented REST path.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition states 'Default target: the REST-style `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` (reads headers ~line 267)' as the monkey-patch target. However, `_call_with_backoff` is the urllib/REST-style path that **already** handles 429 + Retry-After (it catches `urllib.error.HTTPError` and reads `exc.headers.get('Retry-After')`). C4's ticket (lambswool-shaded-eider) explicitly targets the **plain subprocess `_run_acli` path**, which only sees returncode + text stdout/stderr and has no existing 429/Retry-After handling. These are two structurally different code paths. The plan's 'Default target' for monkey-patching C4 would exercise already-implemented behavior, not the new C4 behavior.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4 (429/Retry-After): 'Default target: the REST-style acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff (reads headers ~line 267)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the C4 signal's patch target contingent on C4's probe result (lambswool-shaded-eider AC #1): specify the mock shape for both possible outcomes (HTTPError path via `_call_with_backoff` vs. CalledProcessError path via `_run_acli`) so the harness is correct regardless of which path C4 lands on.
findings.finding: The C4 signal pre-commits to monkey-patching `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` as the 'Default target' for the 429/Retry-After test, but C4's own ticket (lambswool-shaded-eider) explicitly states that Retry-After availability on the subprocess path is UNVERIFIED and that the fix may land on the plain subprocess path instead. The plan asserts a specific patch target before the probe result (C4 AC #1) is known.
findings.location: Signal definitions, C4 entry; lambswool-shaded-eider ticket AC #1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and name the actual production call site(s) that will implement 429/Retry-After after C4 lands (likely `_call_with_retry` in dispatch_one.py or `_run_acli` in acli_subprocess.py) as the C4 monkey-patch target, and remove `_call_with_backoff` as the default — it has no production call sites.
findings.finding: The plan's default C4 monkey-patch target (`acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff`) has zero production call sites in src/ — it is dead/orphaned code used only in one test. Patching it would prove nothing about the real reconciler 429-handling path (which uses `_call_with_retry` in dispatch_one.py and `_run_acli` in acli_subprocess.py). If C4 lands on `_call_with_retry` or `_run_acli`, the harness must patch those instead, but the plan does not identify them as candidates.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 (429/Retry-After): 'Default target: the REST-style `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` (reads headers ~line 267)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the C4 signal's default target with: 'patch the callable that C4 modifies — verify against the C4 diff once landed; likely `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py) or `_run_acli` (acli_subprocess.py)' rather than naming a currently-dead helper.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the C4 conditional patch-target fork with a single unconditional contract: define which module attribute the harness patches (e.g. `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff`) regardless of C4's implementation path, or document an explicit harness probe (e.g. inspect the function signature) that resolves the fork at fixture setup time.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition specifies a conditional patch target with no protocol for resolution: 'Default target: `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` (reads headers ~line 267) … If C4 lands only on the plain subprocess path, patch that.' The harness executor must inspect C4's implementation to choose between two distinct patch targets, but no decision rule or detection protocol is defined for making that choice at harness-build time.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 bullet; acli_subprocess.py:236

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the harness tracks artifact IDs in a mutable list/set populated *before* each create call (not after) so the `finally` block can attempt teardown of every artifact that was attempted, even on partial setup failure.
findings.finding: Partial-setup teardown tracking is unspecified: if harness setup fails after creating K of N synthetic bindings (e.g., network error mid-loop), the `yield`/`finally` fixture must know exactly which Jira issues and local bindings were already created to clean them up. The plan does not specify how the fixture tracks incremental creation state.
findings.location: Harness section — 'pytest autouse fixture yield+finally'; AC line 'Harness with GUARANTEED bilateral teardown'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a pre-registered intent pattern: before each `create_issue` call, append a sentinel to the cleanup list; on success replace with the real ID; on failure the sentinel triggers a no-op or warning in teardown. This ensures partial-setup artifacts are always in scope for cleanup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the coupling to `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` and `acli.py:716-720` as a maintainability risk; propose a stable test seam (e.g., an injectable backoff hook or a named constant for the delete-check line range) and add a `mock.assert_called_once` / symbol-existence guard so a mispatched target fails loudly rather than vacuously.
findings.finding: The harness couples directly to private implementation details — `acli_subprocess._call_with_backoff` by name and approximate line number (~267), and `acli.py:716-720` — without acknowledging this as a coupling risk or proposing a stable test seam/hook. If C4 refactors that code path (or any of the named lines shift), the monkey-patch silently targets the wrong symbol and the C4 scenario passes vacuously.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 scenario; Harness section; AC bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a thin, named test-seam function or config point in `acli_subprocess` (e.g., `_backoff_sleep_fn` injectable at construction) that the harness patches instead of the private call-site. Add `mock_sleep.assert_called()` after the 429 scenario to guarantee the patch was active. For the delete-permission probe, reference a public exception type or a named constant rather than a raw line number.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the blast-radius signal assertion: specify exactly which object fields to assert — e.g. `BindingWalkResult.refused == True` AND `BindingWalkResult.census["breaker"]["allowed"] == False` — not just 'the `refused` census/verdict'.
findings.finding: The blast-radius signal says to assert 'the `refused` census/verdict the breaker emits' — but `refused` is a boolean field on `BindingWalkResult` (binding_walk.py:77), while `census` is a separate dict (classify.py:342-376) that contains a `breaker` sub-dict (with `allowed`, `acting_fraction`, `reason`). The plan conflates two distinct objects (`BindingWalkResult.refused` and `classify.census()["breaker"]`) into a single phrase without specifying which field(s) the harness must assert on.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Blast-radius breaker bullet; binding_walk.py:70-77; classify.py:293-376
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'asserted via the `refused` census/verdict the breaker emits' with: 'assert `walk_result.refused is True` (BindingWalkResult.refused, binding_walk.py:77) AND `walk_result.census["breaker"]["allowed"] is False` (classify.census()["breaker"], classify.py:370).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference and extend the existing `tests/external/` bilateral-cleanup infrastructure (specifically `test_link_sync_live.py`/`test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py` teardown patterns and the `_live_jira_ready()` gate) rather than re-specifying from scratch; note explicitly where the new harness supersedes the existing CLEANUP WARNING pattern.
findings.finding: The plan mandates a bilateral teardown harness using pytest autouse fixture + `yield`+`finally` and a `leaked-artifacts.log` retry mechanism, but does not acknowledge or build on the existing `tests/external/` teardown infrastructure (test_link_sync_live.py, test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py, test_ref_lock_ac0_live.py) that already implements try/finally bilateral Jira cleanup with `delete_issue`. Implementing in isolation risks diverging from established repo conventions and duplicating the credential/gate pattern.
findings.location: Harness section; AC line 1 ('Harness with GUARANTEED bilateral teardown'); tests/external/test_link_sync_live.py:141-154; tests/external/test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py:418-431
findings.suggested_fix: In the Harness section, add: 'Build on the existing `tests/external/` teardown seam (see `test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py:418-431`); the retry+`leaked-artifacts.log` mechanism replaces the bare `print(CLEANUP WARNING)` of existing tests — document this upgrade in the ADR/design note.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the ADR/design-note AC as an observable outcome: specify the file path (e.g. `docs/adr/NNN-live-reconciler-harness.md`), minimum sections (motivation, teardown contract, credential model), and a `grep`-able marker (e.g. `## Teardown Contract`) so the criterion is binary.
findings.finding: The AC 'Harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' is effort-framed ('recorded') with no observable outcome — no minimum content, no location, no format check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria > last bullet ('Runs after the C-band fixes land…')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the report's output path/filename pattern (e.g. `test-results/reconciler-live-matrix.xml`) and the CI upload step (e.g. `actions/upload-artifact`) so the report AC is binary and unambiguous.
findings.finding: The JSON/JUnit report that 'the harness attaches' has no specified location, filename pattern, or schema, making it impossible to verify the report was actually produced post-run.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria > second bullet ('One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario…')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'report written to `test-results/reconciler-live-matrix.xml` (JUnit) and uploaded as a CI artifact named `reconciler-live-matrix`; verified present via `test -f test-results/reconciler-live-matrix.xml && python -c "import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET; ET.parse(\"test-results/reconciler-live-matrix.xml\")"`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define N explicitly in the status round-trip signal (e.g. 'N=3 passes' or 'N = RECONCILER_ABSENT_RETIRE_GRACE default'), matching any existing convention in the test suite.
findings.finding: The status round-trip oscillation check specifies 'No oscillation over N passes' but N is never defined anywhere in the plan. An executor cannot write the harness loop without knowing how many passes constitute a non-oscillation proof.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Status round-trip bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'N=3' (matching the grace default) and cite the existing integration test at tests/integration/rebar_reconciler/test_reconcile_idempotency.py as the pattern to follow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default for N in the oscillation check (e.g. 'N = 3 passes') directly in the signal definition so the executor doesn't have to invent it.
findings.finding: The status-round-trip oscillation check says 'No oscillation over N passes' but N is never defined; an executor must guess how many passes to run.
findings.location: Signal definitions > Status round-trip incl. LOSSY labels
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'No oscillation over 3 passes (N=3)' — consistent with the TOMBSTONE grace-period default already used elsewhere in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to C1's Signal definition (or directly in the plan) the exact `reason` string value and payload key the conflict record will carry when C1 lands, so the harness assertion can be written unambiguously.
findings.finding: The C1 signal definition instructs the harness to 'assert against the exact key/field C1 lands' but C1 (feline-wandering-dassierat) is OPEN/pre-manifest and its ticket AC only says 'emit a conflict record (comment/alert)' — the conflict record's exact payload key and value vocabulary (e.g., what `reason` string is emitted) is never specified. The live code at apply_outbound.py:221 uses `payload.get('reason', 'unspecified')`, but C1 has not yet landed the field that populates this. An executor cannot write the promised assertion without guessing the `reason` value.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C1 bullet; apply_outbound.py:221; ticket feline-wandering-dassierat
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the C1 signal bullet: 'The conflict comment payload carries `reason="both_sides_diverged"` (or the exact value C1 defines at apply_outbound.py); assert `payload["reason"] == <C1-defined-value>` not merely that a conflict comment exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the teardown retry schedule (e.g., exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s with per-call timeout of 10s) so the 'guaranteed cleanup' claim has a concrete SLA.
findings.finding: Teardown retry backoff timing/strategy is unspecified: the plan mandates '3x retries-with-backoff' for cleanup calls but does not define the backoff curve (fixed, linear, exponential), inter-retry delays, or per-call timeout. For a 'guaranteed bilateral cleanup' claim, the retry schedule must be concrete enough to actually outlast transient Jira unavailability.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: each cleanup call retries-with-backoff (3x)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture and storage location of `leaked-artifacts.log` (e.g., written to a non-artifact local path and excluded from CI upload, or explicitly marked internal-only), and confirm it contains only artifact IDs (no token values, no PII) — cite the access class in the harness design note / ADR.
findings.finding: The `leaked-artifacts.log` file introduced by the teardown-failure fallback has an undeclared access posture: the plan does not specify where this file is written (local disk, CI artifact store, shared workspace), who can read it, or whether it is retained/published after the run. In a CI context the file could be uploaded as a public artifact, exposing internal Jira issue keys (project name, issue numbering) to any actor with repo/artifact read access.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: … append the id to `leaked-artifacts.log` and exit non-zero'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ADR/design note: '`leaked-artifacts.log` is written to `$RUNNER_TEMP` (or equivalent per-job temp dir), excluded from CI artifact upload via `.artifactignore` / an explicit upload allow-list, and contains only Jira issue keys (no credentials, no PII). Access class: internal-only, discarded after job.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete negative-assertion pattern for C2, e.g. 'assert no comment body matches /re-created/ (case-insensitive) on the new issue via `client.get_comments(<new_id>)`' so the mechanism is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The C2 signal requires asserting the ABSENCE of a false 're-created' comment, but no assertion mechanism is specified — the harness must know which comment text to reject and how to query it.
findings.location: Signal definitions > C2 (hard-delete -> re-create)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the isolation AC, e.g. `git -C <prod-worktree> diff --exit-code HEAD` (must be zero) and a Jira API query confirming no issues were created outside the TEST project during the run.
findings.finding: The AC 'production REB + tickets branch untouched' has no proving command; there is no described step that would produce evidence this constraint was met.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria > Isolation documented

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a body section (or expand the Harness section) that specifies the ADR/design note: its location (e.g., `docs/adr/`), required content (harness pattern, bilateral teardown approach, isolation strategy), and who owns it — so the final AC bullet is fully covered by described work.
findings.finding: The AC requires 'Harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' but no section of the plan body describes producing, scoping, or placing this ADR/design note — it is an orphan criterion with no corresponding described deliverable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet: 'Harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under Harness titled 'ADR / design note' that states: location (e.g., `docs/adr/NNN-live-reconciler-harness.md`), required sections (motivation, harness pattern, bilateral teardown guarantee, isolation approach, re-runability), and that it must be merged alongside the harness PR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the ProvenanceLedger lifecycle in the harness: specify creation (per-scenario fresh instance or explicit reset), ownership (fixture vs. scenario), and teardown/discard path, ensuring scenarios are isolated from each other's baseline state.
findings.finding: ProvenanceLedger baseline state lifecycle in the harness is underspecified: the plan requires 'harness pre-seeds the baseline exactly as a real pass would' for echo-suppression validation, but does not specify who creates the ProvenanceLedger instance in the harness, whether it is isolated per test scenario or shared across scenarios (concurrency/ordering risk), or how it is cleaned up post-run.
findings.location: Signal definitions — 'Echo suppression' scenario; Isolation section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the Setup Probe rationale: `acli.py:721-723` RAISES `PermissionError` (it does NOT swallow it); revise the stated motivation for the delete-permission probe to the accurate behavior.
findings.finding: The Setup Probe section asserts 'acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError' as justification for requiring the delete-permission probe. Reading acli.py:716-724 shows the opposite: lines 716-720 handle the 404/not_found path (returning {status: not_found}), and lines 721-723 explicitly RAISE PermissionError. The code does NOT swallow it. This false premise undermines the AC's rationale for the probe: if PermissionError is already raised loudly, the stated danger is absent.
findings.location: Setup probe section: 'assert client.delete_issue returns {"status":"deleted"} (NOT a 403 soft-fail — acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError)'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Setup Probe note to: 'assert client.delete_issue returns {"status":"deleted"} (acli.py:721-723 raises PermissionError on 403 — verify the test-project service account has Delete permission so teardown does not raise before any cleanup runs)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the C3 signal definition: the current code is FULLY SILENT (no stderr print) at apply_outbound.py:117-124; update 'today the code only prints to stderr' to 'today the code drops the field silently with no signal'.
findings.finding: The C3 signal definition states 'today the code only prints to stderr' as the current behavior of apply_outbound.py:117-124. Reading that file, the comment at line 118-119 says the fields are 'silently dropped' — there is no print or stderr write. The C3 ticket (nonglacial-tense-vulture) also describes the bug as 'no signal'. The plan's claim about current behavior is factually incorrect: the current code is fully silent (no stderr print), not 'only prints to stderr'.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C3: 'today the code only prints to stderr — assert the post-C3 alert'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(today the code only prints to stderr — assert the post-C3 alert)' with '(today the code drops the field silently at apply_outbound.py:117-124 with no signal; assert the post-C3 BRIDGE_ALERT fires instead)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `acli.py` line citation: PermissionError is **raised** at lines 721-723 (not swallowed at 716-720); update the probe rationale to accurately state that `delete_issue` raises `PermissionError` on 403, making the probe necessary to confirm the service account has the required `Delete Issues` permission before teardown relies on it.
findings.finding: The plan cites `acli.py:716-720` as the location where PermissionError is swallowed ('acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError'), used to justify the delete-permission probe. The actual code at lines 716-720 covers the `subprocess.CalledProcessError` catch and the 404/not_found branch (`return {'status': 'not_found', 'key': jira_key}`). PermissionError is explicitly RAISED at lines 721-723 (`raise PermissionError(msg) from exc`), not swallowed. The cited line range is wrong for the stated purpose — the referenced symbol behavior is hallucinated.
findings.location: Setup probe section — 'assert client.delete_issue returns {"status":"deleted"} (NOT a 403 soft-fail — acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'NOT a 403 soft-fail — acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError' with 'acli.py:721-723 raises PermissionError on 403 — the probe must confirm the service account actually has Delete Issues permission so teardown does not fail with an unhandled PermissionError'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the setup-probe rationale: `acli.py:716-724` raises `PermissionError` on 403 (it does not swallow it); the actual risk the probe guards against is the bare-`except`/print pattern in existing cleanup code — update the cited lines and the stated failure mode accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan's Harness setup-probe section cites `acli.py:716-720` as swallowing a PermissionError, but the live code at lines 721-723 actually RAISES `PermissionError` (it does not swallow it). The conclusion — that a 403 soft-fail must be probed — remains valid, but the cited mechanism is wrong: the probe is needed because `_run_acli` may surface a 403 as a non-zero exit that the CLEANUP WARNING pattern silently prints rather than propagates, not because `delete_issue` itself swallows it.
findings.location: Harness section, Setup probe; acli.py:716-724
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'acli.py:716-720 swallows PermissionError' with: 'the existing `tests/external/` cleanup pattern catches all exceptions and prints a WARNING without re-raising — the probe confirms delete actually succeeds before that silent-discard path is relied on for teardown.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact Jira project role used for the test service account and enumerate (or link to) its full permission set to confirm it is minimally scoped to what the harness actually requires (Create, Browse, Edit, Delete Issues — nothing more, test project only).
findings.finding: The 'Isolation, credentials' section names a service account with 'Delete Issues' scoped to the test project but does not specify the full permission set of the project role — only the delete permission is called out. A project role typically bundles Create, Browse, Edit, Transition, and Comment permissions as well. Without enumerating (or referencing) the exact role used and its full permission set, it is not verifiable that the account is minimally privileged beyond delete.
findings.location: Isolation, credentials section — 'a service account with "Delete Issues" scoped to that project only (project role, not global)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Isolation/credentials section, add: 'The service account is granted the custom project role `[ROLE_NAME]` on the test project, which includes: Browse Projects, Create Issues, Edit Issues, Delete Issues, Add Comments, Transition Issues — no global permissions, no other projects.' Reference the role definition in the Jira admin console or an ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add a prerequisite ticket to build the blast-radius breaker with its `refused` census/verdict interface before this harness runs, or (b) remove the blast-radius scenario from the matrix and AC, since the asserted capability does not exist in the codebase.
findings.finding: The blast-radius breaker matrix scenario asserts a fully-specified runtime feature ('the `refused` census/verdict the breaker emits', 0.09/0.10 fraction thresholds, >=11 synthetic bindings) that does not exist anywhere in the codebase. No implementation of `blast_radius`, `BlastRadius`, `breaker`, `refused`, `census`, `verdict`, `fraction`, or `mass_change` was found in src/ or tests/.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Blast-radius breaker: 'with >=11 synthetic bindings, one acting decision (fraction 0.09) does NOT trip; a synthetic mass-change (> the 0.10 fraction) trips it — asserted via the `refused` census/verdict the breaker emits'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `ProvenanceLedger (differ.py)` with the actual mechanism name/API used for baseline seeding in differ.py, and clarify the concrete harness step for pre-seeding the baseline before the second pass.
findings.finding: The echo-suppression signal references 'ProvenanceLedger (differ.py)' as the baseline-seeding mechanism and asserts `mutation_count==0` as the observable. `ProvenanceLedger` does not exist anywhere in src/ — no class, no module, no attribute by that name. The actual baseline mechanism in differ.py uses a different internal approach. The harness author cannot pre-seed a `ProvenanceLedger` as the plan directs.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Echo suppression: 'field/status writes are baseline-seeded via ProvenanceLedger (differ.py) so they are not re-ingested — verify by `mutation_count==0` on the next pass over the just-written data (harness pre-seeds the baseline exactly as a real pass would)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a ProvenanceLedger pre-seed protocol to the Echo suppression signal: specify the correct module (conflict_resolver.ProvenanceLedger, not differ.py), the constructor arguments, and the exact seeding call sequence the harness must invoke to replicate 'what a real pass would do'.
findings.finding: The echo suppression signal says 'harness pre-seeds the baseline exactly as a real pass would' referencing ProvenanceLedger (differ.py), but never defines the protocol for how the harness creates and retires ProvenanceLedger entries in the throwaway env. ProvenanceLedger is co-located in conflict_resolver.py (a non-obvious module for a signal defined in terms of differ.py), and the harness setup protocol for seeding it is absent.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Echo suppression bullet; conflict_resolver.py:227; differ.py:564

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
